coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots crosslinked cellulose Nanofibril/Chitosan interpenetrating hydrogel system for sensitive detection and efficient adsorption of Cu (II) and Cr (VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xueqi
Song, Zihui
Yuan, Bingnan
Li, Xiaojuan
Li, Shaopeng
Thang Nguyen, Tat
Guo, Minghui
Guo, Zhanhu
coredata.dc:description: To address the pollution of heavy metal ions in water resources, a novel self-assembled nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) crosslinked, cellulose nanofibril (CNF) and chitosan (CS) based interpenetrating polymer network (IPN) hydrogel (NCDs-CNF/CSgel) was prepared for simultaneous fluorescent detection and adsorption of Cu (II) cation and Cr (VI) anion. The NCDs cross-linked CNF hydrogel network was synthesized by an environmentally friendly low-temperature hydrothermal process and subsequently, solvent replacement was used to form the second layer of CS network. The morphology, chemical structure, fluorescent properties, and adsorption behaviors of NCDs-CNF/CSgel were analyzed. The results showed that NCDs-CNF/CSgel exhibited a wide linear range of fluorescence response for Cu (II) (50–1000 mg/L, detection limit of 40.3398 mg/L) and superior sensitivity and selectivity of fluorescence response for Cr (VI) (linear range of 1–50 mg/L, detection limit of 0.7093 mg/L). NCDs-CNF/CSgel showed high adsorption capacities of 148.30 mg/g and 294.46 mg/g for Cu (II) and Cr (VI), respectively, and fitted well to the pseudo-second-order model and the Langmuir model. The detection of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) and their adsorption mechanisms onto NCDs-CNF/CSgel were further determined. Hence, this work provides a green and sustainable approach for the synthesis of a functional material which can be applied for the detection and adsorption of heavy metal ions.

Adsorption studies of Cu (II) and Cr (VI)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of NCDs-CNFgel (a, b) and NCDs-CNF/CSgel (d, e), TEM images of NCDs-CNFgel (c) and NCDs-CNF/CSgel (f).

XRD patterns (a), FT-IR spectra(b), XPS survey spectra(c), TG (f) and DTG (g) curves of CNF, NCDs-CNFgel, NCDs-CNF/CSgel, and CS. High-resolution C 1 s (d) and N 1 s (e) XPS spectra of NCDs-CNFgel and NCDs-CNF/CSgel.

UV–vis absorption spectra (a), PL emission spectra (b) at 350 nm (optimum excitation wavelength) of NCDs solution, NCDs-CNFgel and NCDs-CNF/CSgel. PL emission spectra of NCDs solution (c) and NCDs-CNF/CSgel (d) at different excitation wavelengths. (e) PL intensity of NCDs-CNF/CSgel as a function of solution pH. (f) Digital photos of NCDs-CNF/CSgels in different pH solutions (1.4–7.1) irradiated by 365 nm UV light. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of NCDs-CNF/CSgel and the concentration of Cu (II) (g) and Cr (VI) (h) (the insets on the right side are digital photos of the fluorescent NCDs-CNF/CSgels at different concentrations of heavy metal ions).

(a) Digital photos of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) adsorption processes (heavy metal ion concentration is 1000 mg/L, NCDs-CNF/CSgel sample is about 20 mm in diameter and 5 mm in thickness). Effects of pH (b) and adsorbent types (c) on Cu (II) and Cr (VI) adsorption (heavy metal ion concentration is 100 mg/L, adsorbent dosage is 1 g/L). (d) Fitting Cu (II) and (e) Cr (VI) adsorption data at different initial concentrations based on Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption isotherm models. (f) Fitting Cu (II) and (g) Cr (VI) adsorption data at different adsorption times based on the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models.

Comparison of the FT-IR spectra (a) and XPS survey spectra (b) of NCDs-CNF/CSgels before and after Cu (II) and Cr (VI) adsorption. Cu 2p (c) and Cr 2p (d) XPS high-resolution spectra after adsorption. O 1 s (e), and N 1 s (f) XPS high-resolution spectra of NCDs-CNF/CSgels before and after adsorption.
Construction procedure and formation mechanism of the fluorescent NCDs-CNF/CSgel.
Adsorption and detection mechanisms of NCDs-CNF/CSgels for Cu (II) and Cr (VI).
Isotherm parameters of Langmuir and Freundlich models for the adsorption of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) onto NCDs-CNF/CSgel.
Comparison of the adsorption performances for Cu (II) and Cr (VI) with previous biomass-based adsorbents.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To address the pollution of heavy metal ions in water resources, a novel self-assembled nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) crosslinked, cellulose nanofibril (CNF) and chitosan (CS) based interpenetrating polymer network (IPN) hydrogel (NCDs-CNF/CSgel) was prepared for simultaneous fluorescent detection and adsorption of Cu (II) cation and Cr (VI) anion. The NCDs cross-linked CNF hydrogel network was synthesized by an environmentally friendly low-temperature hydrothermal process and subsequently, solvent replacement was used to form the second layer of CS network. The morphology, chemical structure, fluorescent properties, and adsorption behaviors of NCDs-CNF/CSgel were analyzed. The results showed that NCDs-CNF/CSgel exhibited a wide linear range of fluorescence response for Cu (II) (50–1000 mg/L, detection limit of 40.3398 mg/L) and superior sensitivity and selectivity of fluorescence response for Cr (VI) (linear range of 1–50 mg/L, detection limit of 0.7093 mg/L). NCDs-CNF/CSgel showed high adsorption capacities of 148.30 mg/g and 294.46 mg/g for Cu (II) and Cr (VI), respectively, and fitted well to the pseudo-second-order model and the Langmuir model. The detection of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) and their adsorption mechanisms onto NCDs-CNF/CSgel were further determined. Hence, this work provides a green and sustainable approach for the synthesis of a functional material which can be applied for the detection and adsorption of heavy metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous advancement of the global industrialization process, there are increasing applications of heavy metals in industrial production processes[1]. After various heavy metal minerals are mined and smelted, they are widely used in industrial processes such as metal surface treatment, electronic component processing, machinery manufacturing, printing and dyeing. In these processes, industrial wastewater rich in heavy metal ions is inevitably produced[2]. Due to the wide application of copper (Cu) and chromium (Cr) in industry, the toxic and harmful Cu(II) and Cr(VI) are often present among the heavy metal ions discharged from industrial wastewater[3,4]. Cu(II) and Cr(VI) can be bioaccumulated through the food chain, severely damaging the ecological environment and endangering human health[5,6,7]. For these reasons, the development of efficient and low cost materials for detecting and adsorbing Cu(II) and Cr(VI) in water has become an important research direction in the field of wastewater treatment. In recent years, various adsorbents have been developed for treatment of heavy metal ion pollution in water, including activated carbon[8,9], biochar[10,11], ion exchange resins[12,13], bio-adsorbents[14], polymer brushes[15,16], and hydrogels[17,18].

Hydrogels have a crosslinked three-dimensional network structure that can soak up a large amount of water and maintain a stable shape in water[19]. The hydrogel matrix should have the following characteristics: its surface should be rich in hydrophilic functional groups but it should be insoluble in water, and it should have a chain structure that can be crosslinked or entangled through hydrogen bonds, electrostatic forces or chemical bonds[19,20]. Due to the presence of hydrophilic activated groups on the surface of the hydrogel matrix, Cu(II) and Cr(VI) in the wastewater can electrostatically adsorb, hydrogen bond or chelate with the hydrogel to achieve the purpose of capturing heavy metal ions in the water[5,21]. At the same time, since the hydrogel is insoluble and exists stably in water, it can be easily separated from wastewater, thereby avoiding secondary pollution. Based on these features, hydrogels have gained wide attention as a new type of adsorbents for heavy metal ions. Zhang et al.[22] prepared a soybean dregs–poly(acrylic acid) hydrogel, which was used to adsorb Zn (II), Fe (III), Cu (II), and Cr (III), with adsorption capacities of 121.2, 78.5, 75.4, and 41.7 mg/g, respectively. Arshad et al.[23] used polyethyleneimine modified graphene oxide hydrogel to achieve efficient adsorption of Pb (II), Hg (II) and Cd (II). Lv et al.[24] prepared a poly(acrylic acid) hydrogel adsorbent to remove trace metal ions including Cu (II), Cd (II), Cr (VI), Fe (III), and Mn (II). However, these hydrogel adsorbents also have some shortcomings. The artificial polymers used as the hydrogel matrix are usually non-degradable and biologically toxic, and they generally cannot adsorb amphoteric heavy metal ions. The functional nanomaterials introduced into hydrogel adsorbents are usually costly and hard to be generalized. Based on the above, abundant, sustainable and environmentally-friendly natural biomass is an excellent raw material for preparing hydrogels for application in heavy metal ion adsorption.

As representative natural polysaccharides in biomass, cellulose and chitosan (CS) are chain polymers with abundant hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface[21,25,26]. They can be used as the “skeleton” of hydrogel adsorbents to offer rigid support and provide active sites for heavy metal ion adsorption. However, hydrogels prepared only by physical crosslinking of natural polysaccharides such as chitosan or cellulose have the disadvantages of poor mechanical strength, instability in acidic solutions, and single types of active sites, which restrict their application as adsorbents[27]. Therefore, in recent years, one or more of the following methods have been used to prepare biomass-based hydrogel adsorbents, including: (1) Graft modification of natural polysaccharide polymer chains[28]; (2) Introduction of one or more artificial polymers[29]; (3) Formation of network structures through free radical polymerization of unsaturated ethylenic monomers[30]; (4) Addition of organic or inorganic small molecules as crosslinker[18,31]. For example, Abou-Zeid et al.[32] prepared a hydrogel adsorbent with amino-functionalized Fe3O4 nano-particles as the crosslinker and acrylic acid grafted TEMPO oxidized cellulose nanofibrils as the matrix to adsorb lead ions in water. Wang et al.[17] used freeze–thaw technique to obtain a composite hydrogel adsorbent by forming hydrogen bonds between poly (vinyl alcohol) and carboxymethyl cellulose, which can adsorb Ag (Ⅰ), Ni (II), Cu (II), and Zn (II). Zhou et al.[30] crosslinked microcrystalline cellulose with diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) to develop the cellulose-based amphoteric adsorbent, which can be used to adsorb low-level anionic and cationic heavy metal ions. Vakili et al.[7] prepared a chitosan hydrogel with diepoxyoctane as the crosslinking agent that can stably adsorb Cr(VI) under acidic conditions. However, the introduction of artificial polymers and crosslinkers results in several environmental problems, including dependence on fossil fuels, non-degradability and biotoxicity of the prepared hydrogels. Therefore, it is valuable to exclude artificial polymers and prepare environmentally friendly, degradable composite hydrogel adsorbents with multiple adsorption sites based on different biomass materials.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of fluorescent zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials that can be prepared by simple hydrothermal carbonization of biomass-based polysaccharides, and have the advantages of eco-friendliness, low bio-toxicity and multiple active sites[33,34].In addition, due to the complexation and electron transfer between the surface active functional groups and the graphite-like core of CDs with heavy metal ions, the fluorescence emission of CDs can be selectively quenched by some heavy metal ions[35,36]. The above properties of CDs suggest that they can effectively trap heavy metal ions in water and realize the detection of heavy metal ions by lowering the fluorescence intensity. In order to use and recycle CDs well, previous studies have considered loading CDs on fibers, membranes or gels[37,38]. Zou et al.[39] immobilized CDs on TEMPO oxidized cellulose with a silane coupling agent to detect Hg (II) in water. Lv et al.[40] incorporated the as-prepared CDs into bacterial cellulose membranes through a biosynthesis method, which presented a sensitive fluorescence response to Fe (III).

In this work, two natural polysaccharides, CNF and CS, were employed as the IPN hydrogel skeleton, and CNF based self-assembled NCDs provided CNF crosslinking sites and fluorescence emission. The amino-rich CS provided more active sites and positive charges for improving the chemical adsorption capacity and electrostatic attraction of Cu(II) and Cr(VI). The resulting hydrogel (named as NCDs-CNF/CSgel) was obtained by a facile and mild hydrothermal and solvent replacement method. The detailed experimental procedures, including preparation and characterization methods are described in the Supporting Information. NCDs-CNF/CSgel can be simultaneously used as fluorescent detection material and adsorbent for toxic heavy metal ions Cu(II) and Cr(VI) in water. The morphology, structure, synthesis mechanism and properties of the hydrogel were characterized. The fluorescent responses of the hydrogel and the ion concentrations of Cu(II) and Cr(VI) were analyzed. The adsorption mechanism was explored through adsorption isotherms, adsorption kinetic models, and characterization of the hydrogel before and after adsorption of Cu(II) and Cr(VI). This report can benefit the development of hydrogels for visual monitoring and treatment of ambient heavy metal ions. Moreover, the proposed synthesis and application process of the hydrogel is nontoxic, eco-friendly, and sustainable.

In order to study the morphology of NCDs which were formed during the hydrothermal process, transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images (Figure S1) of NCDs in solution were obtained. NCDs exhibited a dot-like morphology with an average diameter of 2.71 nm (Figure S1 (d)), which were uniformly distributed in the solution (Figure S1 (a)). The crystal lattices of the NCD core can be clearly seen from the HRTEM images (Figure S1 (b), (c)). The measurement result shows that the well-resolved lattice spacing of about 0.21 nm matches with the characteristic of graphite-like carbon[41]. Figure S1 (e) presents the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of NCDs. The broad diffraction peak observed at 2θ = 22.1° further confirms that NCDs possessed graphite-like carbon cores[42,43].

To study the embedding situation of NCDs and network structure of NCDs-CNFgel and NCDs-CNF/CSgel, scanning electron microscope (SEM) and TEM images are shown in Fig. 1 . As shown in Fig. 1 (a), (b), the freeze-dried NCDs-CNFgel possessed a three-dimensional network porous structure. Through further magnification, it can be clearly seen that the skeleton of NCDs-CNFgel was mainly interconnected wall-shaped CNF, with a small amount of filamentous CNF on the surface. The TEM image of NCDs-CNFgel (Fig. 1 (c)) shows that the NCDs were trapped in the CNF hydrogel, and well dispersed without aggregation. However, the crystal lattices of NCDs could not be observed, because NCDs were wrapped in CNF and NCDs-CNF/CSgel presented a dense structure (Fig. 1 (d)). The three-dimensional network structure of the xerogel still existed, but the pore size was reduced significantly. A large number of cobweb-like CS nanofibrils were adhered to the CNF xerogel walls to form the second gel network, which effectively improved the adsorption performance of the composite gel. At the same time, due to the interference of the dense gel structure (Fig. 1 (f)), it was difficult to observe the NCDs in NCDs-CNF/CSgel by TEM, and the shadows of suspended NCDs can only be vaguely seen in the TEM image.

The concentrations of CS hydrosols used for solvent replacement of NCDs-CNFgel greatly influenced the morphology of NCDs-CNF/CSgel, as shown in Figure S2 (a-d). When the concentration of CS hydrosol was 0.5 % (w/v), the morphology of the prepared NCDs-CNF/CS0.5gel (Figure S2 (b)) was similar to that of NCDs-CNFgel (Figure S2 (a)). However, CNF filaments with a large number of carboxylates attracted a lower concentration of amino-rich CS nanofibrils by electrostatic force, and thus formed a thin-walled structure of mixed CNF and CS, which existed on the surface of the gel. When the concentration of CS hydrosol was 1% (w/v), more CS hydrosol entered into the NCDs-CNFgels through solvent displacement, forming a unique cobweb-like CS gel network, which was filled within the CNF gel network (Figure S2 (b)). The SEM image of NCDs-CNF/CS2gel is shown in Figure S2 (d). CS with a higher concentration formed a rough crust on the surface of the NCDs-CNFgel, which blocked most of the pores. It is worth noting that due to excessive viscosity of the CS hydrosol with a concentration of 2 % (w/v), NCDs-CNFgels inevitably generated compressive stress and compaction phenomenon. As a result, compared with NCDs-CNFgel, the diameter and thickness of NCDs-CNF/CS2gel were significantly reduced, and showed a compressed curved surface (inset of Figure S2 (d)).

The XRD patterns of CNF, CS, NCDs-CNFgel, and NCDs-CNF/CSgel are shown in Fig. 2 (a). Both NCDs-CNFgel and NCDs-CNF/CSgel show the characteristic diffraction peaks of typical cellulose I crystalline structure at 2θ = 16, 22.6, and 34.8°[44], which indicates that the hydrothermal treatment and subsequent preparation steps did not destroy the original crystal structure of CNF. As the hydrothermal treatment partially degraded the amorphous regions of CNF and formed NCDs in the process, the crystallinity of NCDs-CNFgel was significantly increased compared with CNF. Moreover, in the pattern of NCDs-CNF/CSgel, the amorphous peak of CS at 20.4° was observed[45], and the peak intensity was significantly weaker than that of CS, indicating that CS was successfully introduced into the hydrogel network structure. CS nanofibrils were uniformly distributed inside NCDs-CNFgel, which led to decreased crystallinity.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CNF, CS, NCDs-CNFgel and NCDs-CNF/CSgel are compared in Fig. 2 (b). The strong bands at around 3300–3500 cm−1 in the spectra can be attributed to the O–H and (or) N–H stretching vibrations. The O–H and N–H bands of NCDs-CNF/CSgel were significantly wider and stronger than those of the other three, due to the stronger intermolecular and intramolecular hydrogen bonding in NCDs-CNF/CSgel. The absorption bands located at 2800–2900 cm−1 in all four spectra were derived from the C–H stretching vibration band. Due to the similar skeleton and chemical structure of CS and CNF, the four spectra with CS or CNF as the main component presented similar fingerprint bands at 1200–850 cm−1, corresponding to the glucopyranose rings of CS and CNF[46,47]. Based on these results, it can be inferred that the low-temperature hydrothermal treatment and immersion gelation process during the preparation of the composite hydrogel did not affect the main chemical structures of CNF and CS.

The FT-IR spectrum of NCDs (Figure S1 (f)) shows the stretching vibration bands of N–H, O–H and C–H around 3300 and 2900 cm−1, respectively. The three absorption bands at 1648, 1560 and 1327 cm−1 correspond to amides Ⅰ, II and III bands, respectively[48]. The characteristic bands of aromatic heterocyclic compounds, namely, the heterocyclic N—(C)3, C—O—C stretching vibration, and the aromatic C–H out-of-plane stretching vibration, were observed at 1385, 1046, and 606 cm−1, respectively[49,50,51]. These results confirmed that EDA recombined with the partially hydrolyzed CNF sugar units during hydrothermal treatment to form NCDs with nitrogen-doped carbonized cores and surface functional groups rich in nitrogen and oxygen[52,53]. The FT-IR spectra of CNF and hydrothermal NCDs-CNFgel were carefully analyzed and compared to confirm the cross-linking state of NCDs and CNF (Fig. 2 (b)). Comparison of the spectra of CNF and NCDs-CNFgel shows that, the C = O stretching band of the carboxyl group of TEMPO-oxidized CNF at 1614 cm−1 [54] was partially covered in the spectrum of NCDs-CNFgel by two new absorption bands at 1635 and 1602 cm−1, which corresponded to amide I and amide II, respectively[40,55]. This indicates that the hydrothermally synthesized amino-rich NCDs formed amide bands through the condensation reaction with the carboxyl groups of CNF, and were then embedded in CNF to serve as the cross-linking agent for NCDs-CNFgel[55]. With the introduction of CS into the composite hydrogel system and the formation of another layer of the gel network, the spectrum of NCDs-CNF/CSgel showed the C—N stretching vibration at 1321 cm−1, which is the characteristic band of CS[56]. Affected by the absorption band of CS at 1646 cm−1 (amide I)[57], the position of the amide I band of NCDs-CNF/CSgel was shifted compared with that of NCDs-CNFgel. However, no new absorption bands appeared in the spectrum of NCDs-CNF/CSgel, implying that CS did not chemically react with other components in the hydrogel system. CS dissolved in acetic acid solution was introduced into NCDs-CNFgel by solvent replacement. During the subsequent treatment in NaOH solution bath, acetic acid in CS sol was neutralized, which destroyed the equilibrium state of CS sol and caused gelation of CS. Therefore, the CS hydrogel network can be considered to be formed by electrostatic force and physical entanglement. The small peaks at 1727 cm−1 in the spectra of NCDs-CNFgel and NCDs-CNF/CSgel can be attributed to the C = O stretching vibration band of carboxylic acid[58,54]. The absorption bands were caused by the protonation of some carboxylate ions on CNF that did not react with NCDs during the exposure to hydrogen from ion-exchange resin.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed for further structural elucidation. The XPS survey spectra of CNF, CS, NCDs-CNFgel and NCDs-CNF/CSgel are presented in Fig. 2 (c-e) and Figure S3. The survey spectrum of NCDs-CNF/CSgel (Fig. 2 (c)) contains C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s peaks. Due to the introduction of EDA and synthesis of NCDs in the CNF hydrothermal treatment process, the N 1 s peak (2.37 %) appeared in the XPS spectrum of the hydrothermally synthesized NCDs-CNFgel. As a result of the formation of the CS gel network with more nitrogen content (N content is 8.55 %) in NCDs-CNF/CSgel, the nitrogen content in the spectrum of NCDs-CNF/CSgel was increased significantly, reaching 5.06 %.

The C 1 s spectrum of NCDs-CNFgel consists of C–C (284.5 eV), C—N (285.7 eV), C—O (286.2 eV), C = O/C = N (287.4 eV) and –COOH (288.2 eV) peaks (Fig. 2 (d)). Compared with CNF (Figure S3 (a)), the C—N peak at 285.7 eV appeared in NCDs-CNFgel spectrum, and the C = O peak of CNF moved from 287.9 eV to 287.4 eV, which can be attributed to the appearance of C = N peak. The N network1s spectrum of NCDs-CNFgel (Fig. 2 (e)) contained the peaks of amino N (398.7 eV), amide N (399.3 eV) and graphite N (401.4 eV). This is consistent with the results of FT-IR analysis, indicating that NCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment, and possessed N-doped carbonized cores and abundant amine and oxygen-containing groups on the surface. Moreover, the presence of amide N on the surface of NCDs-CNFgel reveals the amide cross-linking between NCDs and CNF. The C—N peak in the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 (d)) and the amino N peak in the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 (e)) of NCDs-CNF/CSgel were significantly larger than those of NCDs-CNFgel. This is because CS with larger C—N peak and amino N peak in C 1 s and N 1 s spectra (shown in Figure S3 (b) and (c), respectively) was introduced into NCDs-CNF/CSgel. However, the proportion of amide N peaks in the N 1 s spectra of NCDs-CNFgel and NCDs-CNF/CSgel (27.5% and 26.8%, respectively) was significantly higher than that of CS (13.4%), which further confirms that the amine-rich NCDs and CNF formed a large number of amide bonds. The C 1 s spectra of NCDs-CNFgel and NCDs-CNF/CSgel both showed a –COOH peak at about 288.2 eV, which was absent in the C 1 s spectra of CNF and CS. This is consistent with the FT-IR analysis result. The hydrogen from ion-exchange resin protonates –COO- units on the surface of CNF that were not cross-linked with NCDs, thereby forming a small amount of –COOH groups.

In order to explore the influence of CS concentration in the solvent replacement of NCDs-CNFgel on the chemical structure of NCDs-CNF/CSgel, the FT-IR spectra of NCDs-CNF/CSgels replaced with different concentrations of CS sols were obtained (Figure S2 (e)). The amide band of NCDs-CNF/CS1gel was the strongest, and the peak of the amide II band at 1602 cm−1 was the most obvious, which indicates that the content of CS in NCDs-CNF/CS1gel was the highest. This was also confirmed by the XPS survey spectra (as shown in Figure S2 (f)), where the N element content of NCDs-CNF/CS1gel (5.06%) was higher than that of NCDs-CNF/CS0.5gel (2.93%) and NCDs-CNF/CS2gel (4.71%). Based on the results of the morphology and structure characterization, it was evident that NCDs-CNF/CS1gel had the most superior cobweb-like structure and the highest CS content.

In order to further support the XPS quantitative analysis of elements, the CHN elemental analysis results for CNF, CS, NCDs-CNFgel, and NCDs-CNF/CS(0.5/1/2)gel are shown in Table S1. The elemental analysis data showed a similar trend to that of XPS atomic percentage described above (Fig. 2 (c) and Figure S2 (f)). The increase in the proportion of C element and the appearance of N element (3.14%) in NCDs-CNFgel means that N-doped carbon dots were introduced into the CNF hydrogel system through self-assembly. NCDs-CNF/CS(0.5/1/2)gel was obtained by solvent replacement of NCDs-CNFgel with different concentrations of CS. The results showed that the CS concentration of 1 wt% was the most suitable concentration, and NCDs-CNF/CSgel had the highest N element content (5.58%), indicating that NCDs-CNF/CS1gel had the highest content of CS.

The variation of zeta potentials of CNF, CS, NCDs-CNFgel, and NCDs-CNF/CSgel as a function of pH was determined, as shown in Figure S4. As the pH was increased from 2 to 8, the zeta potential of CNF decreased from −15.7 mV to –32.4 mV which can be attributed to the deprotonation of surface carboxyl groups (weak acidic group) and the formation of –COO- [59,60]. Zeta potential of CS was positive due to the presence of amino groups on the surface. The deprotonation of amino groups caused by the increase in pH lowered the potential of CS from 63.2 mV (pH = 2) to 18.2 mV when pH = 8[61]. In acidic environment, the zeta potential of NCDs-CNFgel was positive, which can be attributed to the presence of amino-rich NCDs on the surface of the hydrogel. However, due to the presence of negatively charged CNF, the absolute value of the zeta potential of NCDs-CNFgel was relatively small. Due to the introduction of CS, zeta potential of NCDs-CNF/CSgel increased compared to NCDs-CNFgel. As the pH increased, the potential of NCDs-CNF/CSgel decreased and reached the zero-point of charge at about pH = 7.8. High negative charge density of NCDs-CNF/CSgel at elevated pH made the negatively charged metal ions more inaccessible.

Thermogravimetric (TG) and DTG analyses of NCDs-CNFgel and NCDs-CNF/CSgel (compared with CNF and CS) were performed. The results are shown in Fig. 2 (f) and (g), respectively. As seen in the TG curves,T CNF was the first one to degrade at a lower temperature (about 200 ℃), and CS began to degrade rapidly at about 250 ℃, while the degradation of NCDs-CNF/CSgel occurred last and was relatively flat. The weight losses corresponding to NCDs-CNFgel and NCDs-CNF/CSgel were 90.35 % and 77.76 %, respectively. The above results indicate that NCDs-CNF/CSgel had better thermal stability. More details of the thermal analysis are shown in the DTG curves. Each sample showed a small degradation peak at low temperature (<150 ℃), which represented the first stage of degradation, that is, the loss of adsorbed water. CNF exhibited two degradation peaks at 257 and 321℃, which represented the degradation of glucose/glucuronate copolymer molecular chains in the amorphous region of CNF and the degradation of CNF in the crystalline region, respectively[62]. After the hydrothermal process, the degradation peaks of NCDs-CNFgel shifted to higher temperatures, forming a weak shoulder peak at 291 ℃ and a stronger degradation peak at 358 ℃. Hence, the thermal stability of NCDs-CNFgel was enhanced[62]. This can be attributed to the hydrothermal process that caused a number of amorphous CNF to react with EDA, resulting in the formation of more thermally stable NCDs. At the same time, NCDs cross-linked with the remaining CNF to form a more orderly and stable chemical structure. The degradation peak of CS at 304 ℃ corresponded to the thermal decomposition of CS, consistent with the results in literature[63]. The degradation peaks of NCDs-CNF/CSgel appeared at 304 ℃ and 358 ℃, corresponding to the degradation peaks of CS and NCDs-CNFgel, respectively. These results confirmed that CS was introduced into the hydrogel system, but no new chemical bonds were formed with NCDs-CNFgel.

The characteristics of the hydrophilic functional groups and hydrogen bonds of the samples can be determined by analyzing the loss peak of adsorbed water, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2 (g). Compared with CNF, the water loss peak of NCDs-CNFgel shifted from 77 to 68 ℃, which confirmed that part of CNF was converted to NCDs by hydrothermal process in NCDs-CNFgel. The N–H bonds of NCDs have weaker water-binding ability than O–H bonds, causing NCDs-CNFgel to lose water at lower temperature. At the same time, due to the chemical cross-linking between NCDs and CNF, a large number of chemical bonds formed, resulting in the increase in ordered structure[64]. Therefore, the groups capable of binding to water were reduced, and the water loss peak of NCDs-CNFgel became smaller and narrower. After CS was introduced into the hydrogel system, it led to a number of active amine groups and hydroxyl groups, but the water loss peak of NCD-CNF/CSgel only slightly increased and broadened, and moved slightly to 72 ℃. This can be attributed to the formation of more intermolecular interactions between CS and NCDs-CNFgel, which reduced the ratio of hydrogen bonds formed by combining with water. As a result, a more stable IPN hydrogel was obtained.

XPS and TG analysis results further support the results of the XRD and FT-IR analyses. The formation mechanism of NCDs-CNF/CSgel is depicted in Scheme 1 . First, hydrothermal treatment promoted the degradation and recombination of part of the CNF under heating and self-pressure, and then formed the fluorescent NCDs with graphite-like carbon cores and surface groups containing N and O on the remaining CNF surface by self-assembly[65,55]. Amino groups on NCDs surface and carboxyl groups on CNF surface were dehydrated and condensed through hydrothermal treatment to form amide bonds for cross-linking NCDs-CNFgel. Subsequently, CS acetic acid sol was introduced into NCDs-CNFgel network by solvent displacement. After treatment in NaOH solution bath, CS sol was neutralized, and gelation of CS sol occurred by electrostatic force and physical entanglement. The second layer of CS gel network was formed on the basis of NCDs-CNFgel, and NCDs-CNF/CSgel with higher surface area and stronger metal ion adsorption capacity was finally obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of NCDs-CNF/CSgel and detection of Cu (II) and Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis and PL spectra were recorded to reveal the optical properties of the NCDs cross-linked hydrogels. In the UV–vis spectra of NCDs solution, NCDs-CNFgel and NCDs-CNF/CSgel (Fig. 3 (a)), shoulder peaks around 230 nm were observed, which can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the conjugated structure of NCDs[66]. The peaks at 260–300 nm were caused by n-π* transition of the nitrogen- or oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of NCDs. As the electrostatic interaction between the carboxyl and amine groups of CNF and CS and the surface groups of NCDs interfered with the energy absorption of the surface groups of NCDs, the peak intensity of the hydrogel was significantly lower than that of the NCDs solution. Furthermore, due to the fibrous morphological structure of CNF and CS in the hydrogels, scattering occurred in the high-wavelength region. Consequently, significant long-tailed absorption at 330–450 nm was observed[55].

Fig. 3 (c) and Fig. 3 (d) show the PL emission spectra of the NCDs solution and NCDs-CNF/CSgel, respectively. The strongest excitation and emission wavelengths of NCDs in solution and hydrogel system were 350 nm and 443 nm, respectively. The emission spectrum of NCDs solution showed a slight excitation wavelength dependence, while NCDs-CNF/CSgel exhibited a stronger excitation wavelength dependence. The best fluorescence emission peak of NCDs-CNF/CSgel was wider than that of NCDs solution (Fig. 3 (b)). From these observations, it can be inferred that the main emission mechanism of NCDs was the energy level recombination of the surface functional groups, rather than the energy level transition of the carbonized cores[67]. The interaction between CNF, CS and NCDs (including cross-linking, physical entanglement and electrostatic interaction) increased the diversity of the surface states of NCDs to a certain extent, and therefore increased the emission peak width of NCDs-CNF/CSgel[55]. There was also a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity because the presence of CNF and CS interfered with the absorption of UV light by NCDs. NCDs-CNF/CSgel exhibited a relatively stable and undisturbed fluorescence emission intensity in the working pH range (around 2–7) of Cu(II) and Cr(VI) adsorption, as shown in Fig. 3 (e) and Fig. 3 (f), which allowed the simultaneous detection of heavy metal ion by the composite hydrogel in acidic condition.

The fluorescence responses of NCDs-CNF/CSgels to Cu (II) and Cr (VI) are exhibited in Fig. 3 (g), (h). Fig. 3 (g) shows that there existed a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9898) between the concentration of Cu (II) and the fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F) of NCDs-CNF/CSgel over a wide concentration range of 50–1000 mg/L. The limit of detection (LOD) of NCDs-CNF/CSgel for Cu (II) was estimated to be 40.3398 mg/L at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The photograph on the right side of the curve shows the fluorescence emission of NCDs-CNF/CSgel under different Cu (II) concentrations, and the fluorescence intensity gradient of the hydrogel can be clearly seen. The curve of Fig. 3 (h) and the photograph on the right-side show that as the concentration of Cr (VI) increased, the fluorescence of NCDs-CNF/CSgel was gradually quenched, and completely quenched when the concentration of Cr (VI) was 200 mg/L. Compared with Cu (II), Cr (VI) had a greater influence on the fluorescence quenching of the hydrogel, and rapid fluorescence quenching occurred in a narrower concentration range. As the Cr (VI) concentration increased from 1 to 50 mg/L, the quenching efficiency increased linearly (R2 = 0.9684, LOD = 0.7093 mg/L). When the concentration exceeded 50 mg/L, the response tended to be stable. This may be attributed to the following two factors: (1) the stronger adsorption capacity of NCDs-CNF/CSgel on Cr (VI), which attracted more Cr (VI) to the vicinity of NCDs; and (2) the stronger electron-capture ability of Cr (VI), which allowed more electrons to transfer from the surface functional groups of NCDs to Cr (VI), resulting in stronger fluorescence quenching[68,69]. These results confirm that NCDs-CNF/CSgel possessed a broad detection range for Cu (II) as well as superior selectivity and sensitivity for Cr (VI).

Figure S5 shows the quenching of different metal ions (with concentrations of 1000 mg/L) by NCDs-CNF/CSgels. Comparatively, Na (Ⅰ), Mg (II), and Zn (II) did not cause obvious fluorescence quenching of NCDs-CNF/CSgel, Cr (VI) caused almost complete fluorescence quenching (99.99 %), while Cu (II), Pb (II), Ca (II), Fe (III), Cd (II), Hg (II), and As (III) caused similar degrees of fluorescence intensity reduction (around 20% to 30%). In brief, NCDs-CNF/CSgel only exhibited fluorecence quenching on heavy metal ions, and Cr (VI) shown the most sensitive and selective fluorescence response. It indicated that NCDs-CNF/CSgel may be used as a heavy metal ions sensor, and achieve a highly sensitive and selective detection of Cr (VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption studies of Cu (II) and Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption process of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) on NCDs-CNF/CSgel is visualized in Fig. 4 (a). Two pieces of hydrogels (diameter ∼ 2 cm, height ∼ 0.5 cm) were placed into 15 mL of 1000 mg/L Cu (II) and Cr (VI) solutions, respectively. After 4 h, the color of the solutions became clear and heavy metal ions were adsorbed. Figure S6 shows the SEM images and energy dispersive X-ray mapping images of the lyophilized hydrogel after adsorption of Cu (II) and Cr (VI), which confirmed that Cu and Cr were adsorbed and distributed on the hydrogel network, respectively. Fig. 4 (b) presents the effect of pH on the adsorption of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) onto NCDs-CNF/CSgel within the pH range of 2 ∼ 5. When the pH range was 2 ∼ 5, copper element mainly existed in the form of Cu2+ and Cu(OH)+. As the pH increased, the content of Cu2+ decreased, and the content of Cu(OH)+ increased. When pH > 5, insoluble Cu(OH)2 began to form, and when the pH reached to above 6.3, copper existed in the form of Cu(OH)2 [70,71]. Cr (VI) existed in several forms in aqueous solution, including HCrO4 -, CrO4 2-, and Cr2O7 2-. CrO4 2- generally appeared at pH > 6. When the pH was in the range of 2 ∼ 6, Cr (VI) existed mainly in the forms of HCrO4 - and Cr2O7 2-. Since the adsorption of Cr (VI) mainly depends on the electrostatic attraction between the adsorbents and Cr (VI) anions, the smaller the pH, the more the adsorption sites and the greater the adsorption capacity[18,71,72]. As seen from the bar chart in the range of pH 2 ∼ 5, the changes in adsorption capacity of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) showed completely opposite trends. The optimal pH values for Cu (II) and Cr (VI) adsorption were 5 and 2, respectively. It is speculated that the increase in pH led to the deprotonation of amino and amide groups and ionization of carboxyl groups of NCDs-CNF/CSgel. Consequently, the cation Cu (II) and the anion Cr (VI) had an opposite tendency to compete for adsorption sites. It is inferred from the above phenomenon that the amino and amide groups provide electrostatic attraction for the adsorption of anion Cr (VI), and the carboxyl groups provide electrostatic attraction for the adsorption of Cu (II). Fig. 4 (c) shows that the adsorption capacity results of different samples were consistent with the results of morphology and chemical structure analyses. When the concentration of CS hydrosol for solvent replacement of NCDs-CNFgel was 1% (w/v), NCDs-CNF/CSgel with the best morphology and properties was obtained.

Fig. 4 (d) and (e) displays the adsorption isotherms of NCDs-CNF/CSgel for Cu (II) and Cr (VI). With the increase in the initial concentration of the heavy metal ions, the adsorption capacity of NCDs-CNF/CSgel gradually increased and eventually tended to be stable. Langmuir (Eqs. (1)) and Freundlich (Eqs. (2)) models were used to fit the equilibrium data, and the specific fitting parameters are listed in Table 1 .

where qe is the adsorption capacity of the samples at equilibrium, qm (mg/g) is the maximum adsorption capacity of the adsorbent. Ce (mg/L) is the concentration at equilibrium in the solution. KL and KF are the constants of Langmuir and Freundlich models, respectively, and 1/n is an empirical constant.

Based on the fitting results, the correlation coefficients (R2) of the Langmuir model fitting curves for both Cu (II) and Cr (VI) were larger than those of the Freundlich model. It can be considered that the adsorption of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) onto NCDs-CNF/CSgel was homogeneous monolayer adsorption[73]. In addition, the maximum adsorption capacities of NCDs-CNF/CSgels for Cu (II) and Cr (VI) simulated by the Langmuir model were 148.30 mg/g and 294.46 mg/g, respectively. The adsorption capacities of NCDs-CNF/CSgels for Cu (II) and Cr (VI) were compared with other biomass-based adsorbents obtained from previous papers, as summarized in Table 2 . The results suggest that NCDs-CNF/CSgel has superior adsorption capacity and can be an effective adsorbent for removing Cu (II) and Cr (VI) from waste water.

To study the effect of contact time on the adsorption capacity, the pseudo-first-order (Eqs. (3)) and pseudo-second-order (Eqs. (4)) kinetic models were fitted to the adsorption isotherms (as shown in Fig. 4(f) and (g)).

where, qe (mg/g) is the theoretical equilibrium adsorption capacity, t is the adsorption time, qt (mg/g) is the adsorption capacity of the samples at time t, k1 (min−1) and k2 (g/ (mg min)) are the rate constants of pseudo-first-order kinetic model and pseudo-second-order kinetic model, respectively.

In the first 4 h, the adsorption capacities of NCDs-CNF/CSgels for Cu (II) and Cr (VI) reached about 90% of the equilibrium adsorption capacity. Subsequently, the increase in adsorption capacities slowed down until the adsorption equilibrium was reached. Table 3 shows the fitting results of the two adsorption kinetic models. The fitting correlation coefficients (R2) of the pseudo-second-order kinetic model for Cu (II) and Cr (VI) adsorption processes were closer to 1 than those of the pseudo-first-order kinetic model, and the theoretical equilibrium adsorption capacities were closer to their measured values. Therefore, the pseudo-second-order kinetic model can better describe the adsorption mechanism, implying that the adsorption processes of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) onto NCDs-CNF/CSgels were chemical adsorption processes with electron transfer or electron pair sharing[26,28]. The k2 corresponding to Cr (VI) adsorption was greater than that corresponding to Cu (II) adsorption, indicating that the adsorption rate of Cr (VI) was slightly greater than that of Cu (II).

To study the thermodynamics of the adsorption process, Van't Hoff' s equations were used to obtain the Gibbs function change (ΔG0), entropy change (ΔS0) and enthalpy change (ΔH0):

where, T (K) is the adsorption temperature, and R is the universal gas constant (8.314 J/ (mol K)).

The estimated thermodynamic parameters are summarized in Table S2. NCDs-CNF/CSgels had negative ΔG0 values for Cu (II) and Cr (VI) adsorption, indicating that the adsorption process was spontaneous, and higher temperatures were beneficial to the adsorption processes[63]. The positive values of ΔH0 meant that the adsorption process of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) was endothermic in general. Since the total entropy values were positive, the freedom degrees of the systems increased, indicating that the adsorption process of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) was entropy-driven processes[63,72].

Recycling experiments were carried out under the optimal adsorption conditions, and the solvents for desorption of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) were selected as EDTA2Na (0.1 M) and NaOH (0.1 M) solutions, respectively. As shown in Figure S7, after five cycles, the adsorption capacity of NCDs-CNF/CSgels remained at about 85% for Cu (II), and remained at about 80% for Cr (VI). The slight decrease in adsorption capacity may be due to the formation of stable complexs, metal hydroxides between NCDs-CNF/CSgels and heavy metal ions. However, the adsorbent still showed great reusability, which is beneficial to its practical application in the field of heavy metal wastewater treatment.

Water samples from Majiagou River (Harbin, P. R. China) and tap water (from local city tap water in Harbin) were collected to evaluate the performance of NCDs-CNF/CSgels in real water treatments. Since the contents of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) in natural water samples are minimal, 10 mg/L of Cu (II) or Cr (VI) was added to the water samples to complete the study. As shown in Figure S8, although the components in river water and tap water are relatively complex and contain small amounts of anions, cations and organic compounds, the residual concentration of Cu (II) or Cr (VI) was about the same. It indicated that the coexisting anions, cations, and organic compounds had no significant effect on the adsorption of Cu (II) and Cr (VI).

FT-IR and XPS analyses of NCDs-CNF/CSgel before and after adsorption of heavy metal ions were performed to study the adsorption mechanisms of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) onto NCDs-CNF/CSgel, respectively. In order to analyze the adsorption mechanisms of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) on NCDs-CNF/CSgels at the optimal pH, the spectra of NCDs-CNF/CSgels before and after adsorption of Cu (II) at pH = 5 as well as before and after adsorption of Cr (VI) at pH = 2 are shown in Fig. 5 (a). As seen from Fig. 5 (a), after Cu (II) and Cr (VI) adsorption, the stretching vibration bands (∼3432 cm−1) of N–H and O–H of the NCDs-CNF/CSgels slightly decreased and shifted to 3396 cm−1 and 3355 cm−1, respectively. This indicated that hydroxyl and amino groups were involved in the adsorption of heavy metal ions as adsorption sites. The characteristic band of glucopyranose rings around 1100 cm−1 was significantly weakened after adsorption, which also confirms that the hydroxyl and amino groups on the structure units of CNF and CS participated in the adsorption process. The C = O stretching vibration band of carboxyl groups at 1733 cm−1 decreased after adsorption, indicating that the carboxyl groups were also the adsorption sites for Cu (II) and Cr (VI). Notably, comparing NCDs-CNF/CSgels before adsorption at pH = 2 and 5, the amide I (1646 cm−1) and amide II (1602 cm−1) bands at pH = 5 were protonated due to the increased acidity, and moved to 1635 and 1527 cm−1 at pH = 2, respectively[82,83]. The amide I and II bands (1646 cm−1 and 1602 cm−1, pH = 5) disappeared after Cu (II) adsorption, and a new sharp band was formed at 1637 cm−1, which can be attributed to the complexation of Cu with amino and amide groups[84]. Analysis of the FT-IR spectra of NCDs-CNF/CSgels before and after adsorption of Cr (VI) at pH = 2 indicated that the amide I and II bands shifted from 1635 and 1527 cm−1 to lower wavenumbers of 1623 cm−1 and 1519 cm−1, and the bands also decreased in intensity, which indicated that chromium ions were adsorbed on the amide sites[85,86]. The typical bands located at 939 cm−1 and 759 cm−1 were assigned to asymmetric Cr—O stretching and asymmetric Cr—O—Cr bridge vibrations, respectively[87,88,89]. The above results verify that Cr (VI) anions were adsorbed on NCDs-CNF/CSgel.

It can be seen from the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 5 (b)) that there were obvious peaks of Cu and Cr elements on the surface of NCDs-CNF/CSgel after adsorption. Fig. 5 (c-f) exhibit the high-resolution regions of Cu 2p, Cr 2p, O 1 s, and N 1 s before and after adsorption of NCDs-CNF/CSgels. The peaks at binding energies of 932.4 and 952.2 eV represent Cu2p 3/2 and Cu2p 1/2 (Fig. 5 (c)). The deconvolution of Cu 2p 3/2 spectrum produced two peaks with binding energies of 933.5 and 932.3 eV. The existence of the three satellite peaks at 962.1, 942.9, and 940.0 eV proves that the copper element mainly existed in the form of Cu (II)[90]. Therefore, the two peaks of Cu 2p 2/3 were respectively assigned to copper hydroxide[91,92,93] and the electron-rich environmental Cu (II). The electron-rich environment was provided by complexation with amino, amide, carboxyl and, hydroxyl groups[94]. This speculation was also corroborated by O 1 s (Fig. 5 (e)) and N 1 s (Fig. 5 (f)) spectra. The O1s spectrum of NCDs-CNF/CSgel with Cu (II) adsorbed presented a new peak at 530.13 eV corresponding to copper hydroxide. The peak area ratio of C—O at 531.9 eV and C = O at 532.5 eV decreased from 1.39 to 0.83. The N 1 s spectrum shifted from 398.8 eV to 399.4 eV, and the intensity of amino peak decreased significantly[95]. This suggests that more electrons of O and N elements were complexed with Cu (II), and the amino, amide, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups participated in the adsorption of Cu (II) as active sites[96,97].

Fig. 5 (d) shows that chromium element existed in two valence states (Cr (III) and Cr (VI)) on the surface of NCDs-CNF/CSgels, indicating that Cr (VI) ions were partially reduced[98,99]. The O 1 s spectrum after the adsorption of Cr (VI) was similar to that after the adsorption of Cu (II), which indicates that the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups participated in the complexation and reduction of Cr (VI), respectively, and part of the reduced Cr (III) formed chromium hydroxide[98]. The N 1 s peak after the adsorption of Cr (VI) shifted from 398.4 eV to 399.2 eV, and a –NO3 peak was observed at 406.3 eV, revealing that amino and amide groups on chitosan and NCDs also participated in the complexation and reduction with Cr (VI). The result was consistent with previous studies. L. Dambies et. al[91] found that in highly acidic solutions (pH < 4), chromate anions were partially chemically reduced (about 60% of Cr (VI)) by raw chitosan beads without crosslinking. R. S. Vieira et. al[100] also confirmed that Cr (VI) is easier to be reduced by the hydroxyl and amino groups of natural and cross-linked chitosan than Cu (II).

Based on the above results, the following mechanisms are proposed (Scheme 2 ) for the adsorption of Cu (II) and Cr (VI) onto NCDs-CNF/CSgels with three-dimensional network: (1) For Cu (II), the complex reaction of the deprotonated amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of NCDs and CS of NCDs-CNF/CSgel with Cu(II) occupied the dominant role of Cu (II) adsorption. In addition, part of Cu (II) were also formed copper hydroxide loaded on NCDs-CNF/CSgel. (2) For Cr (VI), the negatively charged anion Cr (VI) and the protonated NCDs-CNF/CSgel were prone to adsorption by electrostatic attraction. Adsorbed Cr (VI) were partially reduced to Cr (III) and complexed with N and O containing groups of NCDs-CNF/CSgel, and chromium hydroxide loaded on NCDs-CNF/CSgel were also observed.

In addition, heavy metal ions were introduced into NCDs-CNF/CSgels, which shortened the distance between them and NCDs. Consequently, non-radiative energy transfer occurred between the excited electrons of the NCDs’ surface functional groups and heavy metal ions, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Thus, NCDs exhibited sensitive fluorescence response to the two heavy metal ions.

In conclusion, a NCDs-CNF/CSgel with sensing and adsorption properties for heavy metal ions was prepared by a mild and eco-friendly method involving low-temperature hydrothermal treatment and solvent replacement. No toxic small-molecule cross-linkers or artificial polymers were added during the preparation process, and the prepared hydrogel was entirely based on biomass CNF and CS. The morphology, chemical structure, synthesis mechanism, and optical properties of NCDs-CNF/CSgel were analyzed. NCDs-CNF/CSgel exhibited an interpenetrating three-dimensional network structure, in which NCDs served as the cross-linking sites of the NCDs-CNFgel network cross-linked with CNF via amide bonds, and CS formed a second layer of physical hydrogel network. NCDs-CNF/CSgel emitted stable blue-green fluorescence under UV-light excitation. The fluorescence response and adsorption behaviors and mechanisms of NCDs-CNF/CSgel for Cu (II) and Cr (VI) ions were systematically studied. NCDs-CNF/CSgel exhibited a wide linear fluorescence response range for Cu (II) (50–1000 mg/L) and superior sensitivity and selectivity for fluorescence response to Cr (VI) (linear range of 1–50 mg/L), which can be attributed to non-radiative energy transfer between NCDs and heavy metal ions. NCDs-CNF/CSgel showed high adsorption capacites of 148.30 mg/g and 294.46 mg/g for Cu (II) and Cr (VI), respectively, due to the physical and chemical interactions between the N, O containing groups of the hydrogel and heavy metal ions. Overall, the results indicate that NCDs-CNF/CSgel has excellent potential for application in the detection and adsorption of heavy metal ions.
